lists.title: Swahili A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: pronouns, the first noun classes, and the -na-/-li-/-ta- tense system.

lists.title: Swahili A2 Grammar
lists.description: The perfect, objects inside the verb, more noun classes, possession, and commands.

lists.title: Swahili B1 Grammar
lists.description: Subjunctive, negation across tenses, relatives, locatives, and habituals.

lists.title: Swahili B2 Grammar
lists.description: The verb-extension system: passive, causative, applicative, stative, reciprocal.

lists.title: Swahili C2 Grammar
lists.description: Mastery: simultaneous -ki-, idiom families, proverbs, and the registers of respect.

points.title: Personal pronouns and ni (to be)
points.function: Introduce yourself and others
points.explanation: The independent pronouns are mimi (I), wewe (you), yeye (he/she), sisi (we), ninyi (you all), wao (they). 'Ni' is the all-purpose present 'to be' and never changes: Mimi ni mwalimu (I am a teacher), Yeye ni daktari. Negate it with 'si': Mimi si mwalimu.
points.culture_note: Swahili has no grammatical gender at all — yeye is both 'he' and 'she'. Class membership, not gender, is what organizes the language.

drills.translation: I am a teacher.
He/she is not a doctor.
You are a student.
I am not a doctor, I am a teacher.
They are farmers.
Juma is a doctor.

points.title: Subject prefixes (ni-, u-, a-…)
points.function: Make the verb agree with who is acting
points.explanation: Every conjugated verb starts with a subject prefix: ni- (I), u- (you), a- (he/she), tu- (we), m- (you all), wa- (they). The prefix is obligatory even when the pronoun is said: (Mimi) ninasoma. Learn the six person prefixes first — noun classes add more later.

drills.translation: I am reading a book.
They study a lot.
He/she is drinking tea.
We are playing football.
Are you working?
You (plural) are cooking food.

points.title: Present tense -na-
points.function: Say what is happening now or generally
points.explanation: The -na- marker between the subject prefix and the verb stem covers 'am doing' and simple present: ni-na-soma → ninasoma (I am reading / I read), a-na-kula → anakula (she is eating). Structure is always SUBJECT–TENSE–(object)–STEM.

drills.translation: She is drinking tea.
We are learning Swahili.
I am reading a book.
The children are playing football.
Father is working.
You (plural) are drinking tea.

points.title: Noun classes: M-/Wa- (people)
points.function: Talk about people, one or many
points.explanation: Swahili nouns belong to classes marked by prefixes. Class 1/2 covers people: m-/mw- singular, wa- plural — mtu/watu (person/people), mtoto/watoto (child/children), mwalimu/walimu (teacher/s). The class controls agreement on verbs and adjectives.
points.culture_note: The class system replaces gender and articles at once; there are around 15 classes, met a few at a time.

drills.translation: One child, two children.
The teacher is reading.
One person, many people.
The farmers are working in the field.
One student, five students.
The farmer farms the field.

points.title: Noun classes: Ki-/Vi- (things)
points.function: Talk about everyday objects
points.explanation: Class 7/8 mostly covers things: ki- singular, vi- plural — kitabu/vitabu (book/s), kiti/viti (chair/s), kitu/vitu (thing/s). Verbs agree with ki-/vi- for these nouns: Kitabu kinapendeza (The book is pleasing).
points.culture_note: Ki- also makes languages and styles: Kiswahili, Kiingereza (English), kifaransa — 'the Swahili way' extended to 'the Swahili language'.

drills.translation: One book, two books.
The book is on the table.
One chair, three chairs.
One chair is at the table.
Two books are on the table.
One knife, four knives.

points.title: Present negative (si-…-i)
points.function: Say what is not happening
points.explanation: Negate the present with a negative subject prefix and a final -i: si- (I), hu- (you), ha- (he/she), hatu-, ham-, hawa-. The -na- disappears and final -a becomes -i: ninasoma → sisomi (I don't read), anakula → hali (she doesn't eat... from -la).

drills.translation: I don't eat meat.
He doesn't drink coffee.
We don't drink coffee.
drills.hint: to drink + present negative, we (final vowel changes)

drills.translation: Don't you eat meat?
They don't speak English.
The child is not going to school today.

points.title: Past tense -li-
points.function: Say what happened
points.explanation: Swap -na- for -li- for the simple past: nilisoma (I read), walikuja (they came), tulikula (we ate). The rest of the verb is unchanged — Swahili tense lives in that one slot.

drills.translation: Yesterday I went to the market.
They cooked food.
We learned Swahili last year.
She wrote a letter yesterday.
I drank tea in the morning.
The children played football yesterday.

points.title: Future tense -ta-
points.function: Say what will happen
points.explanation: The -ta- marker makes the future: nitasoma (I will read), atakuja (she will come), tutasafiri kesho (we will travel tomorrow).

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to school.
They will arrive tomorrow.
We will go to the market tomorrow.
I will cook ugali in the evening.
He will write a letter tomorrow.
Will you work tomorrow?

points.title: Question words (nini, nani, wapi)
points.function: Ask what, who, and where
points.explanation: Question words usually follow the verb: Unasoma nini? (What are you reading?), Nani anakuja? (Who is coming?), Unaenda wapi? (Where are you going?). Yes/no questions need only rising intonation or an optional opening 'Je,'.

drills.translation: What are you reading?
Where are you going?
Who is coming?
What is your name?
When will you arrive?
Which book do you like?

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Describe people and things
points.explanation: Adjectives take the noun's class prefix: mtoto mzuri (a good child) / watoto wazuri; kitabu kizuri / vitabu vizuri. A small set of adjectives (-zuri good, -baya bad, -kubwa big, -dogo small) covers most A1 needs.

drills.translation: The good children are playing.
The big book is here.
The big house is in town.
The small child is sleeping.
The good books are on the table.
Many people are working.

points.title: Perfect -me-
points.function: Say what has (just) happened
points.explanation: -me- marks a completed action whose result still matters: nimekula (I have eaten), amefika (she has arrived), wamelala (they are asleep — lit. have lain down). It often translates English 'have done' or a present state.

drills.translation: I have eaten.
The guest has arrived.
The children have arrived at school.
We have finished the work.
She has written the letter.
I have already drunk tea.

points.title: Object infixes (-ku-, -m-, -wa-…)
points.function: Say who or what the action is done to
points.explanation: The object goes INSIDE the verb, right before the stem: ni-na-ku-penda → ninakupenda (I love you), a-li-ni-ona → aliniona (she saw me), ni-na-ki-soma → ninakisoma (I am reading it, ki- for a class-7 thing).
points.culture_note: One verb can be a whole sentence: 'Nitakupikia' = I will cook for you.

drills.translation: I love you.
He saw me yesterday.
I saw you yesterday.
He helps them every day.
They are calling us now.
Mother saw him in the morning.

points.title: Noun classes: Ma- (class 5/6)
points.function: Use the ji-/ma- noun family
points.explanation: Class 5/6 nouns take (ji)- in the singular and ma- in the plural: jina/majina (name/s), yai/mayai (egg/s), gari/magari (car/s). Agreement uses li-/ya-: Gari limefika (The car has arrived), Magari yamefika.

drills.translation: One name, many names.
The car has arrived.
One fruit, many fruits.
One egg, five eggs.
The car is on the road.
The cars are here.

points.title: Noun classes: N- (class 9/10)
points.function: Use the N-class — same shape, singular and plural
points.explanation: Class 9/10 nouns look identical in singular and plural: nyumba (house/houses), ndizi (banana/s), simu (phone/s). Agreement tells you the number: Nyumba hii (this house) vs nyumba hizi (these houses); i-/zi- on verbs: Nyumba imejengwa / zimejengwa.

drills.translation: This house is big.
These two houses are big.
These bananas are sweet.
This letter arrived today.
My house is near the market.
The clothes are very attractive.

points.title: Possessives (-angu, -ako, -ake…)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation: Possessive stems are -angu (my), -ako (your), -ake (his/her), -etu (our), -enu (your pl.), -ao (their), and they take the noun's class agreement: kitabu changu (my book), nyumba yangu (my house), watoto wangu (my children).

drills.translation: My book is here.
My house is small.
My children are at school.
Where is your book?
His/her house is far away.
His/her car has arrived.

points.title: The -a of association (wa, ya, cha…)
points.function: Link two nouns ('of')
points.explanation: 'Of' agrees with the FIRST noun: mtoto wa mwalimu (the teacher's child), kitabu cha mtoto (the child's book), nyumba ya Juma (Juma's house), majina ya watoto (the children's names).

drills.translation: The child's book is here.
Juma's house is big.
The teacher's children are here.
The child's name is Juma.
The students' books are on the table.
Mother's clothes are here.

points.title: To have: kuwa na
points.function: Say what people have
points.explanation: 'Have' is 'be with': nina (I have, from ni-na), una, ana, tuna, mna, wana — Nina kitabu (I have a book). Negative: sina, huna, hana… Sina pesa (I have no money). Past: nilikuwa na (I had).

drills.translation: I have a good book.
She has three children.
We have a big house.
Do you have a pen?
They have a farm.
I don't have money, sorry.

points.title: Imperatives and polite requests
points.function: Tell and ask people to do things
points.explanation: The bare stem commands one person: Soma! (Read!), Njoo! (Come!, irregular). Plural adds -eni: Someni! The negative command uses usi-/msi- + -e: Usisome! (Don't read!). Softer requests use the subjunctive: Tafadhali usome (Please read).

drills.translation: Read this book!
Don't make noise!
Come here, please.
Close the door!
Drink a lot of water.
Don't be late to school!

points.title: Subjunctive -e
points.function: Express wants, purposes, and suggestions
points.explanation: Verbs ending in -a switch to -e (no tense marker) after wanting, asking, and purpose: Nataka usome (I want you to read), Alikuja tuzungumze (He came so that we talk), Nifanye nini? (What should I do?).

drills.translation: I want you to read this book.
Let's go to the market to buy fruit.
I want you to return home early.
It is important that we study every day.
Tell him to come here.
What should I do now?

points.title: Negative past and future
points.function: Deny things in any tense
points.explanation: Negative past uses -ku-: sikusoma (I didn't read), hawakuja (they didn't come). Negative future keeps -ta-: sitasoma (I won't read), hatakuja (she won't come). The negative subject prefixes are the same as the present ones.

drills.translation: Yesterday I didn't do work.
She will not come tomorrow.
We didn't go yesterday.
I won't come tomorrow.
They didn't cook food.
Won't you work tomorrow?

points.title: Relative clauses (amba- and -ye-/-o-)
points.function: Say 'who/which' to describe things
points.explanation: Two strategies: amba- plus the class marker — mtu ambaye anasoma (the person who reads), kitabu ambacho nilinunua (the book which I bought) — or the relative infix inside the verb: mtu anayesoma, kitabu nilichonunua. Amba- is easier; the infix is more compact.

drills.translation: The person who is reading is a teacher.
The book that I bought is good.
The house that we bought is big.

drills.sentence: Watu {{answer}} wanafanya kazi hapa ni wazuri.
drills.translation: The people who work here are good.
The person who arrived yesterday is a guest.
drills.hint: to arrive + past with the relative infix, he

drills.translation: The car that I bought is good.

points.title: Locatives (-ni, kuna/pana/mna)
points.function: Talk about places and what is in them
points.explanation: Adding -ni turns a noun into a place: nyumba → nyumbani (at home), shule → shuleni (at school). Existence uses kuna (there is, general), pana (at a definite spot), mna (inside): Shuleni kuna watoto wengi (At school there are many children); negative hakuna.

drills.translation: The children are at school.
Is there food on the table?
The food is on the table.
Is there water inside the bottle?
My parents are at home.
The books are in the bag.

points.title: Habitual hu-
points.function: Talk about routines and general truths
points.explanation: The hu- marker replaces the subject prefix AND the tense for habitual statements: Yeye husoma kila siku (She reads every day), Watu hunywa chai asubuhi (People drink tea in the morning). Context or a noun supplies the subject.

drills.translation: She reads every day.
People drink tea in the morning.
She habitually wakes up early every day.
Farmers till the land in the morning.
We go to the market every week.
Children play football in the evening.

points.title: Kuwa in other tenses (alikuwa, atakuwa)
points.function: Say how things were and will be
points.explanation: 'Ni' has no tense, so other tenses borrow the verb kuwa: alikuwa mwalimu (he was a teacher), nitakuwa daktari (I will be a doctor), tumekuwa marafiki (we have become friends). Same pattern before na: alikuwa na gari (he had a car).

drills.translation: He was a teacher long ago.
I will be a doctor.
We were at school yesterday.
They will be here tomorrow.
Where were you yesterday?
The weather will be good tomorrow.

points.title: Passive -wa
points.function: Say what was done (without the doer)
points.explanation: Insert -w- before the final vowel: kusoma → kusomwa (to be read), kupika → kupikwa. Kitabu kilisomwa (The book was read), Chakula kimepikwa (The food has been cooked). The agent, if said, takes na: kilisomwa na mwalimu.

drills.translation: The food has been cooked.
The letter was written yesterday.
The door has been closed.
The food was cooked by mother.
This work will be done tomorrow.
The thieves were caught yesterday.

points.title: Causative -isha/-esha
points.function: Say you make something happen
points.explanation: The causative extension turns 'do' into 'make do': soma (read) → somesha (teach / make read), lala (sleep) → laza (put to bed, irregular), rudi (return) → rudisha (give back). Mwalimu anawasomesha watoto (The teacher teaches the children).

drills.translation: The teacher teaches the children.
Please return my book.
Mother feeds the child food.
This news will make people happy.
Juma boiled the water.
Please stop the car.
drills.hint: to stand → causative 'bring to a stop'

points.title: Applicative -ia/-ea
points.function: Do something FOR or TO someone
points.explanation: The applicative adds a beneficiary: pika (cook) → pikia (cook for) — Ninakupikia chakula (I am cooking food for you); andika → andikia (write to); nunua → nunulia (buy for). It pairs naturally with an object infix.

drills.translation: Mother is cooking food for the children.
I will write you a letter.
Father bought the children presents.
Please open the door for me.
Mother will bring the guest tea.
My friend wrote me a letter.

points.title: Stative -ika/-eka
points.function: Say something got into a state (or is doable)
points.explanation: The stative describes result-states and possibility without an agent: vunja (break) → vunjika (be/get broken) — Kikombe kimevunjika (The cup is broken); soma → someka (be readable); fanya → fanyika (be doable / happen).

drills.translation: The cup has broken / is broken.
This work is getting done today.
This door opens easily.
This food has gone bad.
His words are understandable.
The glass broke yesterday.

points.title: Reciprocal -ana
points.function: Say people do things to each other
points.explanation: -ana makes the action mutual: penda → pendana (love each other) — Wanapendana (They love each other); ona → onana (see each other / meet) — Tutaonana kesho (We'll see each other tomorrow); saidia → saidiana (help each other).

drills.translation: They love each other very much.
See you tomorrow! (we will see each other)
The friends help each other every day.
We met each other at the market yesterday.
The two siblings look very much alike.
These people understand each other well.

points.title: Conditional -ki- (if/when)
points.function: Make real conditions
points.explanation: -ki- inside the verb means 'if/when (realistic)': Ukisoma, utafaulu (If you study, you will succeed); Mvua ikinyesha, tutabaki nyumbani (If it rains, we'll stay home). The negative counterpart is -sipo-: Usiposoma, hutafaulu.

drills.translation: If you study hard, you will succeed.
If it rains, we will stay home.
If you arrive early, we will leave.
If I get money, I will buy a car.
If the children study hard, they will succeed.
If she cooks food, we will eat together.

points.title: Hypothetical -nge- / -ngali-
points.function: Imagine what would (have) happen(ed)
points.explanation: -nge- is the unreal present/future: Ningekuwa na pesa, ningesafiri (If I had money, I would travel). -ngali- pushes it into the unreal past: Ungalisoma, ungalifaulu (Had you studied, you would have passed). Both clauses carry the marker.

drills.translation: If I had money, I would travel.
Had you studied, you would have passed.
If we woke up early, we would catch the bus.
He would come if he knew.
If you knew, what would you do?
Had it rained, we would have stayed home.

points.title: Narrative -ka-
points.function: Tell a story, action after action
points.explanation: In narration, -ka- chains events after an initial past: Alienda sokoni, akanunua ndizi, akarudi nyumbani (He went to the market, then bought bananas, then returned home). It keeps stories flowing without repeating -li-.
points.culture_note: Listen for -ka- in any Swahili story or news report — it is the backbone of narrative style.

drills.translation: He went to the market and (then) bought bananas.
I got home and then cooked food.
We went to town and saw our friends.
He woke up early and drank tea.
They arrived home and ate food.
I bought a book and began to read.

points.title: Noun class U- (class 11/14: uhuru, ukuta)
points.function: Talk about abstracts and long thin things
points.explanation: U- nouns cover abstract ideas (uhuru freedom, upendo love, ukweli truth) and long or thin objects (ukuta wall, ufunguo key, uso face). Subject concord is u- (ukuta ulianguka), the -a of association is wa, and adjectives take m-/mw- (ukuta mrefu). Plurals jump to the N-class (kuta, funguo, nyuso) with its agreements.
points.culture_note: Uhuru (freedom) is one of the great U-class words of East African political history — Uhuru Park in Nairobi, Uhuru Peak on Kilimanjaro.

drills.translation: Independence was gained in 1961.
The wall fell last night.
I have lost the house key.
A mother's love is great.
Tell the truth at all times.
The keys are on the table.
drills.hint: to be (location) with the N-class plural subject

points.title: Noun class Ku- (class 15: kusoma as a noun)
points.function: Use whole actions as subjects
points.explanation: The infinitive doubles as a noun of class 15: Kusoma ni muhimu (reading is important). As a subject it takes ku- agreements (kunafurahisha), its -a of association is kwa (kucheza kwa watoto), and possessives take kw- (kuimba kwake, her singing).

drills.translation: Her singing pleases me.
Swimming needs a lot of practice.
drills.hint: to swim — the action as a noun

drills.translation: Working hard brings success.
Children's playing is important.
Walking every day helps your health.
Living in town has its costs.

points.title: Place classes 16–18 (mahali pa-, ku-, mu-)
points.function: Agree with places, not just point at them
points.explanation: Swahili has three place classes: pa- for definite spots (mahali pazuri, hapa), ku- for general or distant location (huko, kwetu), and mu- for insides (humo, mna). Kuna/pana/mna — 'there is' — are these three classes wearing -na.

drills.translation: This place is suitable for a picnic.
We sat in a nice spot.
Back home there is peace.
In there it is dark.
Our home is a good place.
There at the farm there was a big tree.

points.title: Demonstratives (huyu / hiki / hii… and -le)
points.function: Point at things in any noun class
points.explanation: 'This' is built as h- + the class vowel pattern (huyu mtoto, hiki kitabu, hii nyumba, hili gari, hivi viatu); 'that' swaps to the class concord + -le (yule, kile, ile, lile, vile, wale). Learn them as the class's fingerprint — the demonstrative always tells you the class.

drills.translation: This child studies well.
Those people come from Kenya.
This book is mine.
This house is very big.
This car is new.
These shoes are yours.
This teacher teaches Swahili.
Those guests come from Tanzania.
This egg is raw.
This room is small.
These books are new.
This pen is mine.

points.title: Comparatives (kuliko, zaidi ya, kama)
points.function: Compare people and things
points.explanation: Swahili compares without changing the adjective: X ni mrefu kuliko Y (X is tall(er) than Y). Zaidi (ya) adds 'more (than)', kama equates (as/like), and the superlative is kuliko wote/zote — 'than all'.

drills.sentence: Mlima Kilimanjaro ni mrefu {{answer}} Mlima Kenya.
drills.translation: Mount Kilimanjaro is taller than Mount Kenya.
This tea is sweeter than that one.
She likes coffee more than tea.
This student is the cleverest of all.
He is as tall as his father.
Today it is hotter than yesterday.

points.title: Relative of time -po- (nilipofika)
points.function: Say when something happened in one word
points.explanation: -po- inside the verb builds 'when'-clauses: ni-li-po-fika = when I arrived. The future uses -taka-po- (utakapomaliza, when you finish); kila ninapokuja = every time I come. One verb does the work of an English clause.

drills.translation: When I arrived home, the rain began.
When you finish the work, call me.
When he was a child, he lived in Mombasa.
We will leave when the sun rises.
drills.hint: to rise + future when-marker, class-5 subject

drills.translation: Every time we meet, we talk a lot.
The moment he saw danger, he ran.

points.title: Relative of manner -vyo- (unavyojua)
points.function: Say how — 'the way that…'
points.explanation: -vyo- builds 'the way/how' clauses: kama unavyojua (as you know), jinsi anavyofikiri (how he thinks). The tenseless form suffixes -vyo directly (upendavyo, as you like). It pairs with jinsi, kama, and kadri.

drills.translation: As you know, life is not easy.
Do as you like.
I don't understand how he thinks.
Write the letter the way I showed you.
Things didn't go the way we planned.
Explain things as they happened.
drills.hint: to happen + past + how-marker, class-6 subject

points.title: Reported speech (kwamba, kuwa, eti)
points.function: Pass on what someone said
points.explanation: Kwamba and kuwa introduce reported statements (alisema kwamba atakuja). Reported commands skip them and use the subjunctive (aliniambia nije — she told me to come). Eti flags hearsay you don't vouch for — often with raised eyebrows.
points.culture_note: Eti carries attitude: repeating a claim with eti signals you find it dubious or cheeky — gossip's favourite word.

drills.translation: He said that he will return tomorrow.
They told me that the meeting has been postponed.
Apparently the teacher didn't come today!
She told me to come early.
drills.hint: to come + subjunctive, I — reported command

drills.translation: The newspaper says that prices will rise.
He replied that he knows nothing.

points.title: Compound tenses (alikuwa akisoma / amesoma)
points.function: Set one action inside another time
points.explanation: Kuwa carries the outer tense and the main verb carries aspect: alikuwa akisoma (was reading — ongoing), alikuwa amesoma (had read — completed), atakuwa amemaliza (will have finished). English needs 'was/had/will have'; Swahili stacks two verbs.

drills.translation: When I arrived, the children had fallen asleep.
drills.hint: to sleep + perfect, they — inside a past frame

drills.translation: This time yesterday, we were cooking food.
This time tomorrow, we will have arrived.
drills.hint: to be + future — the outer tense carrier

drills.translation: When he came in, mother was reading a letter.
Last year, they were living in Europe.
By June, I will have finished the course.
drills.hint: to finish + perfect, I — inside a future frame

points.title: Reversive -ua / -oa (funga → fungua)
points.function: Undo a verb with one suffix
points.explanation: The reversive turns an action into its undoing: funga close → fungua open, vaa wear → vua take off, ficha hide → fichua reveal, funika cover → funua uncover, panga arrange → pangua dismantle. It stacks with the other verb extensions you know.

drills.translation: Please open the door.
When he got home, he took off his shoes.
The journalist revealed a big secret.
After the party, we cleared away the tables.
The child sprang the trap with a stick.
drills.hint: to set (a trap) + reversive + past, he

drills.translation: Unwrap your present now!

points.title: Concessives (ingawa, ijapokuwa, licha ya)
points.function: Concede a point and push past it
points.explanation: Ingawa and ijapokuwa open 'although'-clauses; japo does the same solo; licha ya takes a noun ('despite'); hata hivyo pivots a whole sentence ('even so'). Hata + -ki- makes 'even if' (hata akijaribu).

drills.translation: Although it rained, we travelled.
Despite his efforts, he failed.
He went to work although he was ill.
He is poor; even so, he is generous.
Even if he tries hard, he doesn't succeed.
drills.hint: to try + -ki- after 'even' = even if, he

drills.translation: We will continue even though there are obstacles.

points.title: Emphatic ndi- (ndiye, ndipo, ndivyo)
points.function: Say 'that's exactly the one / when / how'
points.explanation: Ndi- + the class's relative -o builds identity emphasis: ndiye (he's the one), ndipo (that's precisely when/where), ndivyo (that's exactly how), ndicho/ndilo/ndio for the other classes. It's how Swahili clefts a sentence without changing word order.

drills.translation: HE is the one who stole the money.

drills.sentence: Hapa {{answer}} tulipokutana kwa mara ya kwanza.
drills.translation: This is exactly where we met for the first time.
That is just how life is.
This book is exactly the one I'm looking for.
THEY are the ones who helped us.
Today is THE day of decision.

points.title: Negative conditional -sipo- (unless)
points.function: Warn about what happens if not
points.explanation: -sipo- packs 'if…not / unless' into the verb: u-sipo-fanya kazi = unless you work. It slots where -ki- and -po- go and pairs naturally with a future consequence.

drills.translation: Unless you work, you won't be paid.
If we don't leave now, we'll be late.
If it doesn't rain, the harvest will be bad.
If he doesn't come tomorrow, we'll start without him.
If you don't learn from mistakes, you will keep repeating them.
If they don't listen, they won't be able to understand.
drills.hint: to be able + negative future, they

points.title: Purpose clauses: ili + subjunctive
points.function: Say what something is FOR
points.explanation: Ili introduces purpose and the verb after it goes subjunctive: ninasoma ili nifaulu (I study so that I pass). The subject can switch mid-sentence — ili hewa iingie (so that air comes in).

drills.translation: I study hard so that I pass the exam.

drills.sentence: Alinunua simu ili {{answer}} na familia yake.
drills.translation: He bought a phone in order to communicate with his family.
We left early in order to avoid the traffic.

drills.sentence: Kula matunda {{answer}} uwe na afya njema.
drills.translation: Eat fruit so that you have good health.
They planted trees so that the environment be conserved.
drills.hint: to conserve + passive subjunctive, class-6 subject

drills.translation: Open the window so that air comes in.

points.title: Participial -ki- (waliondoka wakiimba)
points.function: Paint two actions in one stroke
points.explanation: Beyond 'if', -ki- marks an action running alongside the main one: waliondoka wakiimba (they left singing), nilimwona akitembea (I saw him walking). English needs '-ing'; Swahili reuses -ki- with the subject repeated on both verbs.

drills.translation: They left singing.
I saw him walking on the road.
He sat quietly, gazing out of the window.
We crossed the river swimming.
Mother came home carrying the luggage.
Don't eat while walking.

points.title: Kupiga idioms (kupiga simu, picha, hodi)
points.function: Sound native with the 'hit' verb
points.explanation: Kupiga (to hit) powers dozens of everyday idioms: kupiga simu (make a call), kupiga picha (take a photo), kupiga kelele (make noise), kupiga hodi (announce yourself at a door), kupiga mbio (run), kupiga pasi (iron). Learn the noun — kupiga does the rest.
points.culture_note: Hodi has no English equivalent: you 'knock' with your voice before entering a home, and the answer is karibu — welcome.

drills.translation: I will call you tomorrow morning.
The tourists took many photos in the park.
The children are making noise; I can't read.
The visitor called 'hodi' at the door.
drills.hint: the word you 'hit' to announce yourself

drills.translation: He runs every morning on the beach.
drills.hint: running — the 'hit' idiom for a run

drills.translation: These clothes need ironing.

points.title: Methali — proverbs and their grammar
points.function: Deploy proverbs the way elders do
points.explanation: Methali compress grammar you know — habitual hu-, class agreement, parallel clauses — into fixed wisdom: Haraka haraka haina baraka (haste has no blessing); Haba na haba hujaza kibaba (little by little fills the measure). Quoting the right one at the right moment is a mark of eloquence.
points.culture_note: Methali are quoted in courtrooms, parliaments, and daladala buses alike; capping an argument with the right proverb can end it.

drills.translation: Haste, haste has no blessing.
Little by little fills the measure.
One not taught by their mother is taught by the world.
One finger cannot crush a louse.
Unity is strength, division is weakness.
Slowly is indeed the way to go.

points.title: Respect register (shikamoo, honorific plural)
points.function: Show respect the Swahili way
points.explanation: Elders are greeted with shikamoo and answer marahaba — there is no opting out politely. Respected singulars take plural concords (babu wamefika — grandfather has (respectfully) arrived), requests soften with naomba + subjunctive, and karibu/karibuni welcomes one or many.
points.culture_note: Skipping shikamoo with an elder reads as rudeness, not informality; the honorific plural (wamefika for one person) is everyday respect, not distance.

drills.translation: The child greeted grandmother: 'Shikamoo!'
Grandmother replied: 'Marahaba, my child.'
Excuse me, may I ask a question.
Guests! Welcome (all of you) to our home.
The elder has (respectfully) just arrived.
drills.hint: to arrive + perfect with the honorific PLURAL concord

drills.translation: May you please give me some water.
drills.hint: to give + subjunctive, you…me — softened request
